AI.method: 1) Any-Resolution Neural Signal Sampler projects diverse brain signals into shared semantic space; 2) DINT attention with Sparse Mixture of Experts backbone manages semantic shifts; 3) Masked Temporal-Frequency Modeling dual-domain pretraining objective reconstructs signals in both time and frequency domains.
AI.result: Brain-OF demonstrates superior performance across diverse downstream tasks after pretraining on a large-scale corpus of around 40 datasets, highlighting benefits of joint multimodal integration and dual-domain pretraining.
AI.conclusion: Brain-OF successfully addresses limitations of single-modality brain foundation models by enabling unified multimodal processing through innovative architectural components and pretraining strategies, advancing the field of brain foundation models.

title: Synthetic Visual Genome 2: Extracting Large-scale Spatio-Temporal Scene Graphs from Videos
summary: We introduce Synthetic Visual Genome 2 (SVG2), a large-scale panoptic video scene graph dataset. SVG2 contains over 636K videos with 6.6M objects, 52.0M attributes, and 6.7M relations, providing an order-of-magnitude increase in scale and diversity over prior spatio-temporal scene graph datasets. To create SVG2, we design a fully automated pipeline that combines multi-scale panoptic segmentation, online-offline trajectory tracking with automatic new-object discovery, per-trajectory semantic parsing, and GPT-5-based spatio-temporal relation inference. Building on this resource, we train TRaSER, a video scene graph generation model. TRaSER augments VLMs with a trajectory-aligned token arrangement mechanism and new modules: an object-trajectory resampler and a temporal-window resampler to convert raw videos and panoptic trajectories into compact spatio-temporal scene graphs in a single forward pass. The temporal-window resampler binds visual tokens to short trajectory segments to preserve local motion and temporal semantics, while the object-trajectory resampler aggregates entire trajectories to maintain global context for objects. On the PVSG, VIPSeg, VidOR and SVG2 test datasets, TRaSER improves relation detection by +15 to 20%, object prediction by +30 to 40% over the strongest open-source baselines and by +13% over GPT-5, and attribute prediction by +15%. When TRaSER's generated scene graphs are sent to a VLM for video question answering, it delivers a +1.5 to 4.6% absolute accuracy gain over using video only or video augmented with Qwen2.5-VL's generated scene graphs, demonstrating the utility of explicit spatio-temporal scene graphs as an intermediate representation.

AI.tldr: SVG2 is a massive panoptic video scene graph dataset with 636K videos, and TRaSER is a new model that generates spatio-temporal scene graphs from videos, achieving significant improvements over baselines.
AI.motivation: Prior spatio-temporal scene graph datasets lack scale and diversity, limiting progress in video understanding. There's a need for larger datasets and better models to generate explicit spatio-temporal scene graphs as intermediate representations for video reasoning tasks.
AI.method: Created SVG2 using automated pipeline with multi-scale panoptic segmentation, trajectory tracking with new-object discovery, semantic parsing, and GPT-5-based relation inference. TRaSER model augments VLMs with trajectory-aligned token arrangement, object-trajectory resampler, and temporal-window resampler to generate scene graphs in single forward pass.
AI.result: TRaSER improves relation detection by +15-20%, object prediction by +30-40% over baselines and +13% over GPT-5, and attribute prediction by +15%. When used for VQA, it provides +1.5 to 4.6% accuracy gain over video-only or Qwen2.5-VL scene graphs.
AI.conclusion: SVG2 enables training of high-quality video scene graph generation models like TRaSER, which demonstrates the utility of explicit spatio-temporal scene graphs as intermediate representations for improving video understanding and reasoning tasks.

title: Planning under Distribution Shifts with Causal POMDPs
comment: To appear at the 36th International Conference on Automated Planning and Scheduling (ICAPS-26)
summary: In the real world, planning is often challenged by distribution shifts. As such, a model of the environment obtained under one set of conditions may no longer remain valid as the distribution of states or the environment dynamics change, which in turn causes previously learned strategies to fail. In this work, we propose a theoretical framework for planning under partial observability using Partially Observable Markov Decision Processes (POMDPs) formulated using causal knowledge. By representing shifts in the environment as interventions on this causal POMDP, the framework enables evaluating plans under hypothesized changes and actively identifying which components of the environment have been altered. We show how to maintain and update a belief over both the latent state and the underlying domain, and we prove that the value function remains piecewise linear and convex (PWLC) in this augmented belief space. Preservation of PWLC under distribution shifts has the advantage of maintaining the tractability of planning via $α$-vector-based POMDP methods.

AI.tldr: A theoretical framework for planning under distribution shifts using causal POMDPs, where environmental changes are modeled as interventions, enabling plan evaluation and domain identification while preserving tractable planning.
AI.motivation: Real-world planning faces challenges from distribution shifts where environment models become invalid as conditions change, causing previously learned strategies to fail. There's a need for planning methods that can handle such shifts in partially observable environments.
AI.method: Proposes using Partially Observable Markov Decision Processes (POMDPs) formulated with causal knowledge. Environmental shifts are represented as interventions on this causal POMDP. The framework maintains and updates beliefs over both latent states and the underlying domain, enabling evaluation of plans under hypothesized changes and active identification of altered environment components.
AI.result: Proves that the value function remains piecewise linear and convex (PWLC) in the augmented belief space that includes both state and domain beliefs. This preservation of PWLC structure maintains the tractability of planning using α-vector-based POMDP methods even under distribution shifts.
AI.conclusion: The causal POMDP framework provides a theoretically sound approach for planning under distribution shifts in partially observable environments, maintaining computational tractability while enabling adaptation to changing conditions through causal intervention modeling.

title: Optimization-Based Behavioral Modeling of Mixers for Frequency Comb OFDM Radar Processing
summary: This paper presents an optimization-based behavioral model for mixers driven by multi-tone local oscillator (LO) signals, considered specifically for frequency comb orthogonal frequency-division multiplexing radar applications. Unlike traditional models, the proposed approach is designed and tested for multi-tone LO excitations. The model uses polynomial nonlinearities for both intermediate frequency and LO ports, supported by spectrum-domain fitting that selectively emphasizes strong intermodulation products. In addition, a polynomial block is introduced to capture input power-dependent phase nonlinearity. The approach is validated using circuit-level simulations and supported by measurements. Radar processing results show the model replicates distortive effects in simulations. The proposed model enables rapid system-level performance estimations and waveform optimization, replacing computationally expensive circuit-level simulations.

AI.tldr: Optimization-based behavioral model for mixers with multi-tone LO signals, validated for frequency comb OFDM radar applications.
AI.motivation: Traditional mixer models are inadequate for multi-tone LO excitations used in frequency comb OFDM radar systems. There's a need for accurate behavioral models that can replace computationally expensive circuit-level simulations for system-level performance estimation and waveform optimization.
AI.method: Uses polynomial nonlinearities for both IF and LO ports with spectrum-domain fitting that selectively emphasizes strong intermodulation products. Introduces a polynomial block to capture input power-dependent phase nonlinearity. Model is validated using circuit-level simulations and measurements.
AI.result: The model successfully replicates distortive effects in radar processing simulations. It enables rapid system-level performance estimations and waveform optimization, effectively replacing computationally expensive circuit-level simulations.
AI.conclusion: The proposed optimization-based behavioral model provides an accurate and efficient alternative to circuit-level simulations for mixers driven by multi-tone LO signals in frequency comb OFDM radar applications, facilitating system-level design and optimization.

title: Hierarchical Multi-Scale Graph Learning with Knowledge-Guided Attention for Whole-Slide Image Survival Analysis
summary: We propose a Hierarchical Multi-scale Knowledge-aware Graph Network (HMKGN) that models multi-scale interactions and spatially hierarchical relationships within whole-slide images (WSIs) for cancer prognostication. Unlike conventional attention-based MIL, which ignores spatial organization, or graph-based MIL, which relies on static handcrafted graphs, HMKGN enforces a hierarchical structure with spatial locality constraints, wherein local cellular-level dynamic graphs aggregate spatially proximate patches within each region of interest (ROI) and a global slide-level dynamic graph integrates ROI-level features into WSI-level representations. Moreover, multi-scale integration at the ROI level combines coarse contextual features from broader views with fine-grained structural representations from local patch-graph aggregation. We evaluate HMKGN on four TCGA cohorts (KIRC, LGG, PAAD, and STAD; N=513, 487, 138, and 370) for survival prediction. It consistently outperforms existing MIL-based models, yielding improved concordance indices (10.85% better) and statistically significant stratification of patient survival risk (log-rank p < 0.05).

AI.tldr: HMKGN is a hierarchical multi-scale graph network for cancer prognosis from whole-slide images that models spatial hierarchies and multi-scale interactions, outperforming existing MIL methods.
AI.motivation: Current attention-based MIL ignores spatial organization, while graph-based MIL relies on static handcrafted graphs. There's a need to capture hierarchical spatial relationships and multi-scale interactions within WSIs for better cancer prognostication.
AI.method: HMKGN enforces hierarchical structure with spatial locality constraints: local cellular-level dynamic graphs aggregate spatially proximate patches within each ROI, and global slide-level dynamic graph integrates ROI-level features. Multi-scale integration combines coarse contextual features from broader views with fine-grained structural representations from local patch-graph aggregation.
AI.result: Evaluated on four TCGA cohorts (KIRC, LGG, PAAD, STAD; N=513, 487, 138, 370). Consistently outperformed existing MIL-based models with 10.85% better concordance indices and statistically significant stratification of patient survival risk (log-rank p < 0.05).
AI.conclusion: HMKGN effectively models hierarchical spatial relationships and multi-scale interactions in WSIs, providing superior cancer prognosis performance compared to existing MIL approaches.

title: Antenna Coding Optimization for Pixel Antenna Empowered Wireless Communication Using Deep Learning with Heterogeneous Multi-Head Selection
comment: 6 pages, 5 figures, accepted by IEEE conferences
summary: Pixel antenna is a promising antenna technology that enables flexible adjustment of radiation characteristics and enhancement of wireless systems through antenna coding. This work proposes a novel deep learning-based antenna coding optimization algorithm. Specifically, the proposed algorithm is supported by a heterogeneous multi-head selection mechanism, whose main idea is to train multiple neural networks based on various coding schemes and select the one that leads to the best system performance. Unlike traditional heuristic searching-based algorithms that require high computational complexity to achieve satisfactory performance, the proposed data-driven deep learning approach can achieve 98\% of the performance achieved by the searching-based algorithms with significantly reduced computational complexity. Results demonstrate that in pixel antenna empowered single-input single-output systems, the proposed algorithm achieves a computational speed 81 times faster than the searching-based algorithm. For more complex pixel antenna empowered multiple-input multiple-output systems, the computational speed is 297 times faster than the existing searching-based algorithm. Benefiting from the high performance and low computational complexity, this algorithm demonstrates the significant potential of pixel antennas as a novel and practical technology to enhance wireless systems.

AI.tldr: Deep learning algorithm optimizes pixel antenna coding with 81-297x faster computation than traditional methods while maintaining 98% performance.
AI.motivation: Pixel antennas enable flexible radiation adjustment through antenna coding, but traditional heuristic search algorithms have high computational complexity that limits practical implementation.
AI.method: Proposes a deep learning-based optimization algorithm using heterogeneous multi-head selection mechanism - trains multiple neural networks on different coding schemes and selects the best performing one.
AI.result: Achieves 98% of traditional search algorithm performance with 81x faster computation for SISO systems and 297x faster for MIMO systems.
AI.conclusion: The algorithm demonstrates pixel antennas' practical potential for wireless enhancement through high performance and low computational complexity.

title: TaCarla: A comprehensive benchmarking dataset for end-to-end autonomous driving
summary: Collecting a high-quality dataset is a critical task that demands meticulous attention to detail, as overlooking certain aspects can render the entire dataset unusable. Autonomous driving challenges remain a prominent area of research, requiring further exploration to enhance the perception and planning performance of vehicles. However, existing datasets are often incomplete. For instance, datasets that include perception information generally lack planning data, while planning datasets typically consist of extensive driving sequences where the ego vehicle predominantly drives forward, offering limited behavioral diversity. In addition, many real datasets struggle to evaluate their models, especially for planning tasks, since they lack a proper closed-loop evaluation setup. The CARLA Leaderboard 2.0 challenge, which provides a diverse set of scenarios to address the long-tail problem in autonomous driving, has emerged as a valuable alternative platform for developing perception and planning models in both open-loop and closed-loop evaluation setups. Nevertheless, existing datasets collected on this platform present certain limitations. Some datasets appear to be tailored primarily for limited sensor configuration, with particular sensor configurations. To support end-to-end autonomous driving research, we have collected a new dataset comprising over 2.85 million frames using the CARLA simulation environment for the diverse Leaderboard 2.0 challenge scenarios. Our dataset is designed not only for planning tasks but also supports dynamic object detection, lane divider detection, centerline detection, traffic light recognition, prediction tasks and visual language action models . Furthermore, we demonstrate its versatility by training various models using our dataset. Moreover, we also provide numerical rarity scores to understand how rarely the current state occurs in the dataset.

AI.tldr: A new CARLA-based autonomous driving dataset with 2.85M frames for diverse Leaderboard 2.0 scenarios, supporting multiple tasks including planning, detection, prediction, and VLMs, with rarity scoring.
AI.motivation: Existing autonomous driving datasets are incomplete - perception datasets lack planning data, planning datasets have limited behavioral diversity, and real datasets lack proper closed-loop evaluation. CARLA Leaderboard 2.0 addresses long-tail problems but existing datasets have limited sensor configurations.
AI.method: Collected over 2.85 million frames using CARLA simulation environment for diverse Leaderboard 2.0 challenge scenarios, designed to support multiple tasks including planning, object detection, lane/centerline detection, traffic light recognition, prediction, and visual language action models.
AI.result: Created a comprehensive dataset that addresses limitations of existing datasets, demonstrated versatility by training various models, and provided numerical rarity scores to understand state occurrence frequency.
AI.conclusion: The new dataset supports end-to-end autonomous driving research by providing comprehensive data for multiple tasks in both open-loop and closed-loop evaluation setups, with added value through rarity scoring for better understanding of scenario distribution.

title: EvoX: Meta-Evolution for Automated Discovery
summary: Recent work such as AlphaEvolve has shown that combining LLM-driven optimization with evolutionary search can effectively improve programs, prompts, and algorithms across domains. In this paradigm, previously evaluated solutions are reused to guide the model toward new candidate solutions. Crucially, the effectiveness of this evolution process depends on the search strategy: how prior solutions are selected and varied to generate new candidates. However, most existing methods rely on fixed search strategies with predefined knobs (e.g., explore-exploit ratios) that remain static throughout execution. While effective in some settings, these approaches often fail to adapt across tasks, or even within the same task as the search space changes over time. We introduce EvoX, an adaptive evolution method that optimizes its own evolution process. EvoX jointly evolves candidate solutions and the search strategies used to generate them, continuously updating how prior solutions are selected and varied based on progress. This enables the system to dynamically shift between different search strategies during the optimization process. Across nearly 200 real-world optimization tasks, EvoX outperforms existing AI-driven evolutionary methods including AlphaEvolve, OpenEvolve, GEPA, and ShinkaEvolve on the majority of tasks.

AI.tldr: EvoX is an adaptive evolution method that jointly evolves candidate solutions and search strategies, outperforming existing AI-driven evolutionary methods across 200+ real-world optimization tasks.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM-driven evolutionary methods use fixed search strategies with static parameters that don't adapt across tasks or as search spaces change over time, limiting their effectiveness.
AI.method: EvoX jointly evolves both candidate solutions and the search strategies used to generate them, enabling continuous adaptation of how prior solutions are selected and varied based on progress.
AI.result: Across nearly 200 real-world optimization tasks, EvoX outperforms existing AI-driven evolutionary methods including AlphaEvolve, OpenEvolve, GEPA, and ShinkaEvolve on the majority of tasks.
AI.conclusion: EvoX demonstrates that adaptive evolution of search strategies alongside solutions enables more effective optimization across diverse tasks by dynamically shifting strategies during the optimization process.

title: LE-NeuS: Latency-Efficient Neuro-Symbolic Video Understanding via Adaptive Temporal Verification
summary: Neuro-symbolic approaches to long-form video question answering (LVQA) have demonstrated significant accuracy improvements by grounding temporal reasoning in formal verification. However, existing methods incur prohibitive latency overheads, up to 90x slower than base VLM prompting, rendering them impractical for latency-sensitive edge deployments. We present LE-NeuS, a latency-efficient neuro-symbolic framework that preserves the accuracy benefits of temporal logic-guided video understanding while drastically reducing inference latency. Our key insight is that the dominant computational bottleneck arises from sequential and dense proposition detection across video frames during automaton construction. We address this through two principled optimizations: (1) CLIP guided two-stage adaptive sampling that exploits visual redundancy to skip semantically similar frames while preserving temporal boundaries, and (2) batched proposition detection that parallelizes VLM inference across temporal windows. Theoretically, we derive latency bounds as a function of video length, proposition complexity, and sampling density, establishing conditions under which latency efficiency is achievable. Empirically, on LongVideoBench and Video-MME benchmarks deployed on NVIDIA H100 GPUs, LE-NeuS reduces the latency gap from 90x to approximately 10x while maintaining >10% accuracy gains on temporally complex queries.

AI.tldr: LE-NeuS is a latency-efficient neuro-symbolic framework for long-form video QA that reduces inference latency from 90x to 10x slower than base VLM prompting while maintaining accuracy gains.
AI.motivation: Existing neuro-symbolic approaches to LVQA have prohibitive latency overheads (up to 90x slower than base VLM prompting), making them impractical for latency-sensitive edge deployments, despite their accuracy benefits.
AI.method: Two optimizations: (1) CLIP-guided two-stage adaptive sampling to skip semantically similar frames while preserving temporal boundaries, and (2) batched proposition detection that parallelizes VLM inference across temporal windows. Also provides theoretical latency bounds analysis.
AI.result: On LongVideoBench and Video-MME benchmarks (NVIDIA H100 GPUs), LE-NeuS reduces latency gap from 90x to ~10x while maintaining >10% accuracy gains on temporally complex queries.
AI.conclusion: LE-NeuS demonstrates that neuro-symbolic temporal reasoning can be made latency-efficient through principled optimizations, enabling practical deployment while preserving accuracy benefits for complex temporal queries.

title: Construct, Merge, Solve & Adapt with Reinforcement Learning for the min-max Multiple Traveling Salesman Problem
summary: The Multiple Traveling Salesman Problem (mTSP) extends the Traveling Salesman Problem to m tours that start and end at a common depot and jointly visit all customers exactly once. In the min-max variant, the objective is to minimize the longest tour, reflecting workload balance. We propose a hybrid approach, Construct, Merge, Solve & Adapt with Reinforcement Learning (RL-CMSA), for the symmetric single-depot min-max mTSP. The method iteratively constructs diverse solutions using probabilistic clustering guided by learned pairwise q-values, merges routes into a compact pool, solves a restricted set-covering MILP, and refines solutions via inter-route remove, shift, and swap moves. The q-values are updated by reinforcing city-pair co-occurrences in high-quality solutions, while the pool is adapted through ageing and pruning. This combination of exact optimization and reinforcement-guided construction balances exploration and exploitation. Computational results on random and TSPLIB instances show that RL-CMSA consistently finds (near-)best solutions and outperforms a state-of-the-art hybrid genetic algorithm under comparable time limits, especially as instance size and the number of salesmen increase.

AI.tldr: RL-CMSA: A hybrid reinforcement learning approach for min-max mTSP that combines probabilistic clustering with learned q-values, route merging, exact MILP solving, and local refinement to balance workload across multiple salesmen.
AI.motivation: The min-max mTSP aims to minimize the longest tour to achieve workload balance among multiple salesmen, which is important for practical applications like delivery routing and workforce management. Existing methods need improvement for larger instances and more salesmen.
AI.method: Hybrid approach combining reinforcement learning with exact optimization: 1) Construct diverse solutions using probabilistic clustering guided by learned pairwise q-values, 2) Merge routes into a compact pool, 3) Solve restricted set-covering MILP, 4) Refine solutions via inter-route remove, shift, and swap moves, 5) Update q-values by reinforcing city-pair co-occurrences in high-quality solutions, 6) Adapt pool through ageing and pruning.
AI.result: RL-CMSA consistently finds (near-)best solutions and outperforms a state-of-the-art hybrid genetic algorithm under comparable time limits, especially as instance size and number of salesmen increase. Tested on random and TSPLIB instances.
AI.conclusion: The combination of exact optimization and reinforcement-guided construction effectively balances exploration and exploitation for the min-max mTSP, demonstrating superior performance over existing methods for larger and more complex instances.

title: Design of a Hands-Free Short-Range Intercommunication Device Using LoRa for Secure Field Communication
summary: Short-range reliable and secure communication is a major priority in the tactical, military and disaster response settings where the traditional communication infrastructure is either off-line or prone to interception. Current VHF/UHF radios and software-defined radios are popular but large-sized devices and require lots of power, making them not suitable to be used as lightweight wearable devices with seamless hand-free use. In this paper, the design and theoretical framework of a miniature, LoRa based encrypted intercommunication device that can be used in secure field communication over a range of 1-1.5km and under line-of-sight conditions is provided. The suggested system consists of a voice-activated acquisition block, digital audio compression, an embedded microcontroller processor, and AES-128 encryption followed by a low-power transmission via the LoRa protocol. Through the ability of chirp spread spectrum modulation to utilize the long-range and low-energy properties, the system is guaranteed reliable communications coupled with low power consumption and low electromagnetic footprint. The theoretical analysis of the proposed communication range is justified using a link-budget that justifies the practicability of the communication range in the real propagation conditions. This architecture focuses on infrastructural agnosticism, peer-to-peer security as well as wearable ergonomics. The given scheme shows the possibilities of LoRa technology in the scope of other traditional IoT telemetry, and it can be further extended to include secure tactical voice communication platforms.

AI.tldr: Design of a miniature, LoRa-based encrypted intercommunication device for secure field communication over 1-1.5km range under line-of-sight conditions.
AI.motivation: Need for short-range reliable and secure communication in tactical, military, and disaster response settings where traditional infrastructure is unavailable or vulnerable to interception. Current VHF/UHF radios are too large and power-hungry for lightweight wearable applications.
AI.method: System design includes voice-activated acquisition, digital audio compression, embedded microcontroller processing, AES-128 encryption, and low-power transmission via LoRa protocol using chirp spread spectrum modulation. Theoretical analysis uses link-budget calculations to justify communication range.
AI.result: Proposed system achieves reliable communications with low power consumption and low electromagnetic footprint over 1-1.5km range under line-of-sight conditions, demonstrating LoRa's potential beyond traditional IoT telemetry.
AI.conclusion: The architecture enables infrastructural agnosticism, peer-to-peer security, and wearable ergonomics, showing promise for secure tactical voice communication platforms and extending LoRa technology applications.

title: Unsupervised Causal Prototypical Networks for De-biased Interpretable Dermoscopy Diagnosis
summary: Despite the success of deep learning in dermoscopy image analysis, its inherent black-box nature hinders clinical trust, motivating the use of prototypical networks for case-based visual transparency. However, inevitable selection bias in clinical data often drives these models toward shortcut learning, where environmental confounders are erroneously encoded as predictive prototypes, generating spurious visual evidence that misleads medical decision-making. To mitigate these confounding effects, we propose CausalProto, an Unsupervised Causal Prototypical Network that fundamentally purifies the visual evidence chain. Framed within a Structural Causal Model, we employ an Information Bottleneck-constrained encoder to enforce strict unsupervised orthogonal disentanglement between pathological features and environmental confounders. By mapping these decoupled representations into independent prototypical spaces, we leverage the learned spurious dictionary to perform backdoor adjustment via do-calculus, transforming complex causal interventions into efficient expectation pooling to marginalize environmental noise. Extensive experiments on multiple dermoscopy datasets demonstrate that CausalProto achieves superior diagnostic performance and consistently outperforms standard black box models, while simultaneously providing transparent and high purity visual interpretability without suffering from the traditional accuracy compromise.

AI.tldr: CausalProto is an unsupervised causal prototypical network that purifies visual evidence in dermoscopy analysis by disentangling pathological features from environmental confounders, improving both diagnostic accuracy and interpretability.
AI.motivation: Deep learning's black-box nature hinders clinical trust in dermoscopy analysis, and prototypical networks can suffer from shortcut learning where environmental confounders are encoded as predictive prototypes, generating misleading visual evidence.
AI.method: Uses Structural Causal Model framework with Information Bottleneck-constrained encoder for unsupervised orthogonal disentanglement of pathological features from environmental confounders. Maps decoupled representations to independent prototypical spaces and uses learned spurious dictionary for backdoor adjustment via do-calculus, transforming causal interventions into efficient expectation pooling.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on multiple dermoscopy datasets show CausalProto achieves superior diagnostic performance, consistently outperforms standard black box models, and provides transparent, high-purity visual interpretability without accuracy compromise.
AI.conclusion: CausalProto effectively mitigates confounding effects in dermoscopy analysis by purifying the visual evidence chain through causal disentanglement, offering both improved accuracy and trustworthy interpretability for clinical decision-making.

title: Weighted Unequal Error Protection over a Rayleigh Fading Channel
summary: We study a variant of unequal error protection in channel coding, where the message bit string is divided into a finite number of blocks and the maximization objective is a weighted sum of per-block decoding success probabilities. The channel model is quasi-static Rayleigh fading with channel state information available to the receiver but unavailable to the transmitter. We analyze the asymptotic and finite blocklength performance of two achievability schemes, one based on power-domain superposition (PDS) and another based on orthogonal resource allocation (ORA), also known as time-sharing. Upper bounds on the optimal number of blocks to transmit are derived. Algorithms to compute the optimal power and time splits for the two schemes are given. Simplified algorithms to compute locally optimal power and time splits are also given, and their outputs match those of the previous algorithms across all tested parameters. Our results show that PDS outperforms ORA, but the performance differential is less than 2% in both the asymptotic and finite blocklength regimes (Figures 4 - 6). For both PDS and ORA, numerical results also upper bound the gap between the asymptotic and finite blocklength performance by approximately 10% for n = 1000 and 3% for n = 5000 (Figures 7 - 10).

AI.tldr: This paper studies unequal error protection in quasi-static Rayleigh fading channels, comparing power-domain superposition (PDS) and orthogonal resource allocation (ORA) schemes for maximizing weighted sum of per-block decoding success probabilities.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to address unequal error protection in wireless communications where different message blocks may have different importance levels, and to analyze performance under practical channel conditions (quasi-static Rayleigh fading with receiver-only CSI).
AI.method: Two achievability schemes are analyzed: power-domain superposition (PDS) and orthogonal resource allocation (ORA/time-sharing). The paper derives upper bounds on optimal number of blocks, provides algorithms for optimal power/time splits, and develops simplified algorithms for locally optimal solutions.
AI.result: PDS outperforms ORA but the performance difference is less than 2% in both asymptotic and finite blocklength regimes. The gap between asymptotic and finite blocklength performance is bounded by approximately 10% for n=1000 and 3% for n=5000.
AI.conclusion: Both PDS and ORA are effective for unequal error protection, with PDS having slight performance advantage. The small performance gap suggests ORA may be preferable in practice due to simpler implementation, while the bounded finite blocklength performance gap provides practical design guidelines.

title: V-MORALS: Visual Morse Graph-Aided Estimation of Regions of Attraction in a Learned Latent Space
summary: Reachability analysis has become increasingly important in robotics to distinguish safe from unsafe states. Unfortunately, existing reachability and safety analysis methods often fall short, as they typically require known system dynamics or large datasets to estimate accurate system models, are computationally expensive, and assume full state information. A recent method, called MORALS, aims to address these shortcomings by using topological tools to estimate3DR-eEgnciodnesr of Attraction (ROA) in a low-dimensional latent space. However, MORALS still relies on full state knowledge and has not been studied when only sensor measurements are available. This paper presents Visual Morse Graph-Aided Estimation of Regions of Attraction in a Learned Latent Space (V- MORALS). V-MORALS takes in a dataset of image-based trajectories of a system under a given controller, and learns a latent space for reachability analysis. Using this learned latent space, our method is able to generate well-defined Morse Graphs, from which we can compute ROAs for various systems and controllers. V-MORALS provides capabilities similar to the original MORALS architecture without relying on state knowledge, and using only high-level sensor data. Our project website is at: https://v-morals.onrender.com.

AI.tldr: V-MORALS extends MORALS to estimate Regions of Attraction from image-based trajectories using learned latent spaces, eliminating the need for full state information.
AI.motivation: Existing reachability analysis methods require known system dynamics or large datasets, are computationally expensive, and assume full state information. MORALS addressed some issues but still relies on full state knowledge and hasn't been studied with sensor-only data.
AI.method: V-MORALS learns a latent space from image-based trajectories of a system under a given controller, then generates Morse Graphs in this learned latent space to compute Regions of Attraction without relying on state knowledge.
AI.result: V-MORALS provides capabilities similar to the original MORALS architecture but works with only high-level sensor data (images) instead of requiring full state information.
AI.conclusion: V-MORALS enables reachability analysis for systems where only sensor measurements are available, extending the applicability of topological methods for safety analysis in robotics.

title: Human Supervision as an Information Bottleneck: A Unified Theory of Error Floors in Human-Guided Learning
comment: Proceedings from IEEE CAI 2026, Conference on Artificial Intelligence, 8-10 May, Granada, Spain. 8 Pages, 3 Figures, 7 Tables
summary: Large language models are trained primarily on human-generated data and feedback, yet they exhibit persistent errors arising from annotation noise, subjective preferences, and the limited expressive bandwidth of natural language. We argue that these limitations reflect structural properties of the supervision channel rather than model scale or optimization. We develop a unified theory showing that whenever the human supervision channel is not sufficient for a latent evaluation target, it acts as an information-reducing channel that induces a strictly positive excess-risk floor for any learner dominated by it. We formalize this Human-Bounded Intelligence limit and show that across six complementary frameworks (operator theory, PAC-Bayes, information theory, causal inference, category theory, and game-theoretic analyses of reinforcement learning from human feedback), non-sufficiency yields strictly positive lower bounds arising from the same structural decomposition into annotation noise, preference distortion, and semantic compression. The theory explains why scaling alone cannot eliminate persistent human-aligned errors and characterizes conditions under which auxiliary non-human signals (e.g., retrieval, program execution, tools) increase effective supervision capacity and collapse the floor by restoring information about the latent target. Experiments on real preference data, synthetic known-target tasks, and externally verifiable benchmarks confirm the predicted structural signatures: human-only supervision exhibits a persistent floor, while sufficiently informative auxiliary channels strictly reduce or eliminate excess error.

AI.tldr: Human supervision creates fundamental limits on AI performance due to information loss in human feedback channels, which scaling alone cannot overcome.
AI.motivation: Large language models trained on human data exhibit persistent errors despite scaling, suggesting fundamental limitations in human supervision channels rather than model capacity.
AI.method: Developed unified theory across six frameworks (operator theory, PAC-Bayes, information theory, causal inference, category theory, game theory) showing human supervision acts as information-reducing channel, creating positive excess-risk floor.
AI.result: Theory explains why scaling can't eliminate human-aligned errors; experiments confirm human-only supervision has persistent error floor while auxiliary non-human signals reduce/eliminate excess error.
AI.conclusion: Human supervision has inherent information limits; overcoming persistent errors requires auxiliary non-human signals to restore information about latent evaluation targets.

title: No Calibration, No Depth, No Problem: Cross-Sensor View Synthesis with 3D Consistency
summary: We present the first study of cross-sensor view synthesis across different modalities. We examine a practical, fundamental, yet widely overlooked problem: getting aligned RGB-X data, where most RGB-X prior work assumes such pairs exist and focuses on modality fusion, but it empirically requires huge engineering effort in calibration. We propose a match-densify-consolidate method. First, we perform RGB-X image matching followed by guided point densification. Using the proposed confidence-aware densification and self-matching filtering, we attain better view synthesis and later consolidate them in 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS). Our method uses no 3D priors for X-sensor and only assumes nearly no-cost COLMAP for RGB. We aim to remove the cumbersome calibration for various RGB-X sensors and advance the popularity of cross-sensor learning by a scalable solution that breaks through the bottleneck in large-scale real-world RGB-X data collection.

AI.tldr: First study of cross-sensor view synthesis across different modalities, proposing a match-densify-consolidate method to eliminate cumbersome calibration for RGB-X data collection.
AI.motivation: Existing RGB-X work assumes aligned data pairs exist and focuses on modality fusion, but calibration requires huge engineering effort. The paper aims to remove cumbersome calibration for various RGB-X sensors and advance cross-sensor learning popularity.
AI.method: Match-densify-consolidate approach: 1) RGB-X image matching with guided point densification using confidence-aware densification and self-matching filtering, 2) Consolidation in 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS). Uses no 3D priors for X-sensor and only assumes nearly no-cost COLMAP for RGB.
AI.result: Method attains better view synthesis and provides a scalable solution that breaks through the bottleneck in large-scale real-world RGB-X data collection.
AI.conclusion: Proposes a practical solution to eliminate calibration requirements for RGB-X sensors, enabling scalable cross-sensor learning and advancing the field by overcoming data collection bottlenecks.

title: SleepLM: Natural-Language Intelligence for Human Sleep
summary: We present SleepLM, a family of sleep-language foundation models that enable human sleep alignment, interpretation, and interaction with natural language. Despite the critical role of sleep, learning-based sleep analysis systems operate in closed label spaces (e.g., predefined stages or events) and fail to describe, query, or generalize to novel sleep phenomena. SleepLM bridges natural language and multimodal polysomnography, enabling language-grounded representations of sleep physiology. To support this alignment, we introduce a multilevel sleep caption generation pipeline that enables the curation of the first large-scale sleep-text dataset, comprising over 100K hours of data from more than 10,000 individuals. Furthermore, we present a unified pretraining objective that combines contrastive alignment, caption generation, and signal reconstruction to better capture physiological fidelity and cross-modal interactions. Extensive experiments on real-world sleep understanding tasks verify that SleepLM outperforms state-of-the-art in zero-shot and few-shot learning, cross-modal retrieval, and sleep captioning. Importantly, SleepLM also exhibits intriguing capabilities including language-guided event localization, targeted insight generation, and zero-shot generalization to unseen tasks. All code and data will be open-sourced.

AI.tldr: SleepLM is a sleep-language foundation model that aligns natural language with multimodal sleep data, enabling language-based sleep analysis, interpretation, and interaction beyond traditional closed-label systems.
AI.motivation: Current learning-based sleep analysis systems are limited to predefined labels (stages/events) and cannot describe, query, or generalize to novel sleep phenomena. There's a need for more flexible, language-grounded approaches to sleep analysis.
AI.method: 1) Developed multilevel sleep caption generation pipeline to create first large-scale sleep-text dataset (100K+ hours from 10K+ individuals); 2) Created unified pretraining objective combining contrastive alignment, caption generation, and signal reconstruction; 3) Built SleepLM family of models bridging natural language and multimodal polysomnography.
AI.result: SleepLM outperforms state-of-the-art in zero-shot/few-shot learning, cross-modal retrieval, and sleep captioning. Exhibits novel capabilities: language-guided event localization, targeted insight generation, and zero-shot generalization to unseen tasks.
AI.conclusion: SleepLM successfully bridges natural language with sleep physiology, enabling more flexible and interpretable sleep analysis. The model demonstrates strong performance on real-world tasks and novel capabilities for sleep understanding. All code and data will be open-sourced.

title: Hypercomplex Phase Retrieval
comment: 21 pages, 4 figures, 2 tables. arXiv admin note: substantial text overlap with arXiv:2310.17660
summary: Hypercomplex signal processing (HSP) offers powerful tools for analyzing and processing multidimensional signals by explicitly exploiting inter-dimensional correlations through Clifford algebra. In recent years, hypercomplex formulations of the phase retrieval (PR) problem, wheren a complex-valued signal is recovered from intensity-only measurements, have attracted growing interest. Hypercomplex phase retrieval (HPR) naturally arises in a range of optical imaging and computational sensing applications, where signals are often modeled using quaternion- or octonion-valued representations. Similar to classical PR, HPR problems may involve measurements obtained via complex, hypercomplex, Fourier, or other structured sensing operators. These formulations open new avenues for the development of advanced HSP-based algorithms and theoretical frameworks. This chapter surveys emerging methodologies and applications of HPR, with particular emphasis on optical imaging systems.

AI.tldr: Survey of hypercomplex phase retrieval (HPR) methods and applications, focusing on optical imaging systems that use quaternion/octonion representations to exploit inter-dimensional correlations.
AI.motivation: Hypercomplex signal processing offers powerful tools for analyzing multidimensional signals by exploiting inter-dimensional correlations through Clifford algebra. There's growing interest in hypercomplex formulations of phase retrieval problems, which naturally arise in optical imaging and computational sensing applications where signals are modeled using quaternion- or octonion-valued representations.
AI.method: Survey methodology examining emerging HPR techniques that extend classical phase retrieval to hypercomplex domains. These formulations involve measurements obtained via complex, hypercomplex, Fourier, or other structured sensing operators, opening new avenues for HSP-based algorithms and theoretical frameworks.
AI.result: The chapter provides a comprehensive overview of hypercomplex phase retrieval methodologies and their applications, with particular emphasis on optical imaging systems where these techniques show significant promise.
AI.conclusion: Hypercomplex phase retrieval represents an important advancement in signal processing, offering enhanced capabilities for multidimensional signal analysis in optical imaging and computational sensing applications through the exploitation of inter-dimensional correlations in quaternion/octonion representations.

authors: Udaya S. K. P. Miriya Thanthrige
title: VideoPulse: Neonatal heart rate and peripheral capillary oxygen saturation (SpO2) estimation from contact free video
comment: 11 pages, 3 figures, 5 tables. Preprint. Intended for submission to an IEEE Journal
summary: Remote photoplethysmography (rPPG) enables contact free monitoring of vital signs and is especially valuable for neonates, since conventional methods often require sustained skin contact with adhesive probes that can irritate fragile skin and increase infection control burden. We present VideoPulse, a neonatal dataset and an end to end pipeline that estimates neonatal heart rate and peripheral capillary oxygen saturation (SpO2) from facial video. VideoPulse contains 157 recordings totaling 2.6 hours from 52 neonates with diverse face orientations. Our pipeline performs face alignment and artifact aware supervision using denoised pulse oximeter signals, then applies 3D CNN backbones for heart rate and SpO2 regression with label distribution smoothing and weighted regression for SpO2. Predictions are produced in 2 second windows. On the NBHR neonatal dataset, we obtain heart rate MAE 2.97 bpm using 2 second windows (2.80 bpm at 6 second windows) and SpO2 MAE 1.69 percent. Under cross dataset evaluation, the NBHR trained heart rate model attains 5.34 bpm MAE on VideoPulse, and fine tuning an NBHR pretrained SpO2 model on VideoPulse yields MAE 1.68 percent. These results indicate that short unaligned neonatal video segments can support accurate heart rate and SpO2 estimation, enabling low cost non invasive monitoring in neonatal intensive care.

AI.tldr: VideoPulse: A neonatal dataset and pipeline for contact-free heart rate and SpO2 estimation from facial video using 3D CNNs with artifact-aware supervision.
AI.motivation: Conventional vital sign monitoring for neonates requires adhesive probes that can irritate fragile skin and increase infection risk. Remote photoplethysmography (rPPG) offers contact-free monitoring but needs specialized neonatal datasets and methods.
AI.method: End-to-end pipeline with face alignment, artifact-aware supervision using denoised pulse oximeter signals, 3D CNN backbones for heart rate and SpO2 regression, label distribution smoothing, and weighted regression for SpO2. Predictions in 2-second windows.
AI.result: Heart rate MAE: 2.97 bpm (2-second windows), 2.80 bpm (6-second windows) on NBHR dataset. SpO2 MAE: 1.69%. Cross-dataset evaluation shows 5.34 bpm MAE for heart rate on VideoPulse, and fine-tuned SpO2 model achieves 1.68% MAE.
AI.conclusion: Short unaligned neonatal video segments can support accurate heart rate and SpO2 estimation, enabling low-cost, non-invasive monitoring in neonatal intensive care units.

title: Finite Block Length Rate-Distortion Theory for the Bernoulli Source with Hamming Distortion: A Tutorial
summary: Lossy data compression lies at the heart of modern communication and storage systems. Shannon's rate-distortion theory provides the fundamental limit on how much a source can be compressed at a given fidelity, but it assumes infinitely long block lengths that are never realized in practice. We present a self-contained tutorial on rate-distortion theory for the simplest non-trivial source: a Bernoulli$(p)$ sequence with Hamming distortion. We derive the classical rate-distortion function $RD = Hp - HD$ from first principles, illustrate its computation via the Blahut-Arimoto algorithm, and then develop the finite block length refinements that characterize how the minimum achievable rate approaches the Shannon limit as the block length $n$ grows. The central quantity in this refinement is the \emph{rate-distortion dispersion} $V(D)$, which governs the $O(1/\sqrt{n})$ penalty for operating at finite block lengths. We accompany all theoretical developments with numerical examples and figures generated by accompanying Python scripts.

AI.tldr: A tutorial on rate-distortion theory for Bernoulli sources with Hamming distortion, covering classical results, computational methods, and finite block length refinements including rate-distortion dispersion.
AI.motivation: Shannon's rate-distortion theory provides fundamental compression limits but assumes infinite block lengths, which are impractical. There's a need to understand how compression performance approaches these theoretical limits with finite block lengths in practice.
AI.method: Derives classical rate-distortion function for Bernoulli(p) sources with Hamming distortion from first principles, illustrates computation via Blahut-Arimoto algorithm, and develops finite block length refinements using rate-distortion dispersion V(D).
AI.result: Presents the classical RD = Hp - HD formula, shows how to compute it algorithmically, and characterizes the O(1/√n) penalty for finite block lengths through rate-distortion dispersion analysis.
AI.conclusion: Provides a comprehensive tutorial bridging classical rate-distortion theory with practical finite block length considerations, with numerical examples and Python code to illustrate the concepts.

title: Tilt-X: Enabling Compliant Aerial Manipulation through a Tiltable-Extensible Continuum Manipulator
comment: Accepted to IEEE International Conference on Robotics and Automation (ICRA) 2026
summary: Aerial manipulators extend the reach and manipulation capabilities of uncrewed multirotor aerial vehicles for inspection, agriculture, sampling, and delivery. Continuum arm aerial manipulation systems offer lightweight, dexterous, and compliant interaction opportunities. Existing designs allow manipulation only below the UAV which restricts their deployability in multiple directions and through clutter. They are also sensitive to propeller downwash. Addressing these limitations, we present Tilt-X, a continuum arm aerial manipulator that integrates a tilting mechanism, a telescopic stage, and a cable-driven continuum section. We present its design and kinematic model and validate it through flight demonstrations. Tilt-X enables a volumetric workspace with up to 75 mm extension and planar orientations between 0$^\circ$ to 90$^\circ$. Experiments comparing end effector pose with and without downwash quantitatively measure its accuracy, providing critical evidence to guide the design and control of reliable aerial manipulators. Results show stabilisation of end effector pose as the manipulator extends out of the propeller influence zone.

AI.tldr: Tilt-X is a continuum arm aerial manipulator with tilting mechanism, telescopic stage, and cable-driven continuum section that enables volumetric workspace and reduces propeller downwash interference.
AI.motivation: Existing aerial manipulators are limited to manipulation below UAVs, restricting deployability in multiple directions and through clutter, and are sensitive to propeller downwash.
AI.method: Developed Tilt-X with integrated tilting mechanism, telescopic stage, and cable-driven continuum section; presented design, kinematic model, and validated through flight demonstrations.
AI.result: Achieved volumetric workspace with up to 75 mm extension and planar orientations between 0° to 90°; experiments showed stabilization of end effector pose as manipulator extends out of propeller influence zone.
AI.conclusion: Tilt-X addresses limitations of existing aerial manipulators by enabling multi-directional deployment and reducing downwash interference, providing critical evidence for reliable aerial manipulator design and control.

title: Global Interpretability via Automated Preprocessing: A Framework Inspired by Psychiatric Questionnaires
summary: Psychiatric questionnaires are highly context sensitive and often only weakly predict subsequent symptom severity, which makes the prognostic relationship difficult to learn. Although flexible nonlinear models can improve predictive accuracy, their limited interpretability can erode clinical trust. In fields such as imaging and omics, investigators commonly address visit- and instrument-specific artifacts by extracting stable signal through preprocessing and then fitting an interpretable linear model. We adopt the same strategy for questionnaire data by decoupling preprocessing from prediction: we restrict nonlinear capacity to a baseline preprocessing module that estimates stable item values, and then learn a linear mapping from these stabilized baseline items to future severity. We refer to this two-stage method as REFINE (Redundancy-Exploiting Follow-up-Informed Nonlinear Enhancement), which concentrates nonlinearity in preprocessing while keeping the prognostic relationship transparently linear and therefore globally interpretable through a coefficient matrix, rather than through post hoc local attributions. In experiments, REFINE outperforms other interpretable approaches while preserving clear global attribution of prognostic factors across psychiatric and non-psychiatric longitudinal prediction tasks.

AI.tldr: REFINE is a two-stage method that uses nonlinear preprocessing to stabilize questionnaire data followed by linear prediction, achieving better accuracy than interpretable approaches while maintaining global interpretability through coefficient matrices.
AI.motivation: Psychiatric questionnaires are context-sensitive and weakly predictive of future symptoms. While nonlinear models can improve accuracy, their lack of interpretability undermines clinical trust. The paper aims to develop a method that combines predictive power with interpretability.
AI.method: REFINE decouples preprocessing from prediction: 1) A nonlinear preprocessing module estimates stable item values from questionnaire data, 2) A linear model maps these stabilized items to future symptom severity. This concentrates nonlinearity in preprocessing while keeping the prognostic relationship transparently linear.
AI.result: REFINE outperforms other interpretable approaches while preserving clear global attribution of prognostic factors across psychiatric and non-psychiatric longitudinal prediction tasks. It provides interpretability through a coefficient matrix rather than post hoc local attributions.
AI.conclusion: The REFINE framework successfully addresses the trade-off between predictive accuracy and interpretability in psychiatric questionnaire analysis by separating nonlinear preprocessing from linear prediction, enabling both improved performance and clinical trust through transparent coefficient-based interpretation.

title: Evidential Neural Radiance Fields
summary: Understanding sources of uncertainty is fundamental to trustworthy three-dimensional scene modeling. While recent advances in neural radiance fields (NeRFs) achieve impressive accuracy in scene reconstruction and novel view synthesis, the lack of uncertainty estimation significantly limits their deployment in safety-critical settings. Existing uncertainty quantification methods for NeRFs fail to capture both aleatoric and epistemic uncertainty. Among those that do quantify one or the other, many of them either compromise rendering quality or incur significant computational overhead to obtain uncertainty estimates. To address these issues, we introduce Evidential Neural Radiance Fields, a probabilistic approach that seamlessly integrates with the NeRF rendering process and enables direct quantification of both aleatoric and epistemic uncertainty from a single forward pass. We compare multiple uncertainty quantification methods on three standardized benchmarks, where our approach demonstrates state-of-the-art scene reconstruction fidelity and uncertainty estimation quality.

AI.tldr: Evidential Neural Radiance Fields (ENeRF) is a probabilistic approach that quantifies both aleatoric and epistemic uncertainty in 3D scene modeling using a single forward pass, achieving state-of-the-art reconstruction fidelity and uncertainty estimation.
AI.motivation: Current NeRF methods lack proper uncertainty estimation, which limits their deployment in safety-critical applications. Existing uncertainty quantification methods either fail to capture both types of uncertainty (aleatoric and epistemic), compromise rendering quality, or require significant computational overhead.
AI.method: Evidential Neural Radiance Fields - a probabilistic approach that integrates seamlessly with the NeRF rendering process and enables direct quantification of both aleatoric and epistemic uncertainty from a single forward pass.
AI.result: State-of-the-art scene reconstruction fidelity and uncertainty estimation quality on three standardized benchmarks, outperforming existing uncertainty quantification methods.
AI.conclusion: The proposed Evidential Neural Radiance Fields successfully addresses the limitations of existing NeRF uncertainty methods by providing efficient, high-quality uncertainty quantification without compromising rendering performance, enabling more trustworthy 3D scene modeling for safety-critical applications.

title: MMKG-RDS: Reasoning Data Synthesis via Deep Mining of Multimodal Knowledge Graphs
summary: Synthesizing high-quality training data is crucial for enhancing domain models' reasoning abilities. Existing methods face limitations in long-tail knowledge coverage, effectiveness verification, and interpretability. Knowledge-graph-based approaches still fall short in functionality, granularity, customizability, and evaluation. To address these issues, we propose MMKG-RDS, a flexible framework for reasoning data synthesis that leverages multimodal knowledge graphs. It supports fine-grained knowledge extraction, customizable path sampling, and multidimensional data quality scoring. We validate MMKG-RDS with the MMKG-RDS-Bench dataset, covering five domains, 17 task types, and 14,950 samples. Experimental results show fine-tuning Qwen3 models (0.6B/8B/32B) on a small number of synthesized samples improves reasoning accuracy by 9.2%. The framework also generates distinct data, challenging existing models on tasks involving tables and formulas, useful for complex benchmark construction. The dataset and code are available at https://github.com/360AILAB-NLP/MMKG-RDS

AI.tldr: MMKG-RDS is a flexible framework for synthesizing high-quality reasoning training data using multimodal knowledge graphs, addressing limitations in existing methods for long-tail knowledge coverage, effectiveness verification, and interpretability.
AI.motivation: Existing methods for synthesizing training data to enhance domain models' reasoning abilities have limitations in long-tail knowledge coverage, effectiveness verification, and interpretability. Knowledge-graph-based approaches also fall short in functionality, granularity, customizability, and evaluation.
AI.method: Proposes MMKG-RDS, a flexible framework for reasoning data synthesis leveraging multimodal knowledge graphs. It supports fine-grained knowledge extraction, customizable path sampling, and multidimensional data quality scoring.
AI.result: Validated with MMKG-RDS-Bench dataset covering 5 domains, 17 task types, and 14,950 samples. Fine-tuning Qwen3 models (0.6B/8B/32B) on synthesized samples improved reasoning accuracy by 9.2%. Framework generates challenging data for tasks involving tables and formulas, useful for complex benchmark construction.
AI.conclusion: MMKG-RDS effectively addresses limitations of existing methods and provides a practical framework for synthesizing high-quality reasoning training data using multimodal knowledge graphs, with demonstrated improvements in model performance and utility for benchmark construction.

title: From Signals to Causes: A Causal Signal Processing Framework for Robust and Interpretable Clinical Risk Prediction
summary: Learning-based signal processing systems increasingly support high-stakes medical decisions using heterogeneous biomedical signals, including medical images, physiological time series, and clinical records. Despite strong predictive performance, many models rely on statistical correlations that are unstable across acquisition settings, patient populations, and institutional practices, limiting robustness, interpretability, and clinical trust. We advocate a causal signal processing perspective in which biomedical signals are treated as effects of latent generative mechanisms rather than as isolated predictive inputs. Using clinical risk prediction as a motivating example, we show how disease-related factors generate observable biomarkers, while acquisition processes act as confounders influencing signal appearance. In clinical disease risk prediction from chest CT scans and patient risk factors, correlational models may fail under scanner changes, whereas causal abstractions remain invariant. Building on this view, we propose a unifying conceptual framework integrating causal modeling with learning-based signal processing and neuro-symbolic reasoning. Statistical models extract multimodal representations that are mapped to interpretable causal abstractions and combined with symbolic knowledge encoding clinical risk factors and guidelines. This structure enables clinically grounded explanations, counterfactual reasoning about hypothetical interventions, and improved robustness to distribution shifts arising from changes in acquisition conditions or screening policies. Rather than introducing a specific algorithm, this article presents schematic causal structures and a comparative analysis of correlation-based, causal, and neuro-symbolic approaches to guide the design of robust and interpretable medical decision-support systems.

AI.tldr: The paper advocates for a causal signal processing approach in medical AI, treating biomedical signals as effects of latent generative mechanisms rather than isolated predictive inputs, to improve robustness and interpretability across clinical settings.
AI.motivation: Current learning-based medical systems rely on statistical correlations that are unstable across different acquisition settings, patient populations, and institutional practices, limiting robustness, interpretability, and clinical trust.
AI.method: Proposes a unifying conceptual framework integrating causal modeling with learning-based signal processing and neuro-symbolic reasoning. Statistical models extract multimodal representations that are mapped to interpretable causal abstractions and combined with symbolic knowledge encoding clinical risk factors and guidelines.
AI.result: Causal abstractions remain invariant under scanner changes where correlational models fail. The framework enables clinically grounded explanations, counterfactual reasoning about hypothetical interventions, and improved robustness to distribution shifts from changes in acquisition conditions or screening policies.
AI.conclusion: The paper presents schematic causal structures and comparative analysis to guide design of robust, interpretable medical decision-support systems, advocating for a shift from correlation-based to causal approaches in biomedical signal processing.

title: Breaking the Data Barrier: Robust Few-Shot 3D Vessel Segmentation using Foundation Models
summary: State-of-the-art vessel segmentation methods typically require large-scale annotated datasets and suffer from severe performance degradation under domain shifts. In clinical practice, however, acquiring extensive annotations for every new scanner or protocol is unfeasible. To address this, we propose a novel framework leveraging a pre-trained Vision Foundation Model (DINOv3) adapted for volumetric vessel segmentation. We introduce a lightweight 3D Adapter for volumetric consistency, a multi-scale 3D Aggregator for hierarchical feature fusion, and Z-channel embedding to effectively bridge the gap between 2D pre-training and 3D medical modalities, enabling the model to capture continuous vascular structures from limited data. We validated our method on the TopCoW (in-domain) and Lausanne (out-of-distribution) datasets. In the extreme few-shot regime with 5 training samples, our method achieved a Dice score of 43.42%, marking a 30% relative improvement over the state-of-the-art nnU-Net (33.41%) and outperforming other Transformer-based baselines, such as SwinUNETR and UNETR, by up to 45%. Furthermore, in the out-of-distribution setting, our model demonstrated superior robustness, achieving a 50% relative improvement over nnU-Net (21.37% vs. 14.22%), which suffered from severe domain overfitting. Ablation studies confirmed that our 3D adaptation mechanism and multi-scale aggregation strategy are critical for vascular continuity and robustness. Our results suggest foundation models offer a viable cold-start solution, improving clinical reliability under data scarcity or domain shifts.

AI.tldr: A novel framework using pre-trained Vision Foundation Model (DINOv3) adapted for volumetric vessel segmentation with 3D adapter, multi-scale aggregator, and Z-channel embedding, achieving significant improvements in few-shot and out-of-distribution settings.
AI.motivation: State-of-the-art vessel segmentation methods require large annotated datasets and suffer from performance degradation under domain shifts, which is impractical in clinical settings where acquiring extensive annotations for every new scanner/protocol is unfeasible.
AI.method: Proposed framework leverages pre-trained Vision Foundation Model (DINOv3) adapted for volumetric vessel segmentation with: 1) lightweight 3D Adapter for volumetric consistency, 2) multi-scale 3D Aggregator for hierarchical feature fusion, and 3) Z-channel embedding to bridge the gap between 2D pre-training and 3D medical modalities.
AI.result: In extreme few-shot regime (5 training samples): achieved Dice score of 43.42% (30% relative improvement over nnU-Net's 33.41%, outperforming Transformer baselines by up to 45%). In out-of-distribution setting: achieved 21.37% vs nnU-Net's 14.22% (50% relative improvement). Ablation studies confirmed 3D adaptation and multi-scale aggregation are critical.
AI.conclusion: Foundation models offer a viable cold-start solution for vessel segmentation, improving clinical reliability under data scarcity or domain shifts, with the proposed 3D adaptation mechanisms being critical for capturing continuous vascular structures from limited data.

title: VCA: Vision-Click-Action Framework for Precise Manipulation of Segmented Objects in Target Ambiguous Environments
summary: The reliance on language in Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models introduces ambiguity, cognitive overhead, and difficulties in precise object identification and sequential task execution, particularly in environments with multiple visually similar objects. To address these limitations, we propose Vision-Click-Action (VCA), a framework that replaces verbose textual commands with direct, click-based visual interaction using pretrained segmentation models. By allowing operators to specify target objects clearly through visual selection in the robot's 2D camera view, VCA reduces interpretation errors, lowers cognitive load, and provides a practical and scalable alternative to language-driven interfaces for real-world robotic manipulation. Experimental results validate that the proposed VCA framework achieves effective instance-level manipulation of specified target objects. Experiment videos are available at https://robrosinc.github.io/vca/.

AI.tldr: VCA replaces language commands with click-based visual interaction for robotic manipulation, reducing ambiguity and cognitive load when dealing with multiple similar objects.
AI.motivation: Language-based VLA models suffer from ambiguity, cognitive overhead, and difficulties in precise object identification, especially with multiple visually similar objects. Textual commands create interpretation errors and are impractical for real-world robotic manipulation.
AI.method: Proposes Vision-Click-Action (VCA) framework that replaces textual commands with direct click-based visual interaction using pretrained segmentation models. Operators specify target objects by clicking on them in the robot's 2D camera view.
AI.result: Experimental results validate that VCA achieves effective instance-level manipulation of specified target objects. The framework reduces interpretation errors and lowers cognitive load compared to language-driven interfaces.
AI.conclusion: VCA provides a practical and scalable alternative to language-driven interfaces for real-world robotic manipulation by enabling clear visual specification of target objects through direct interaction.

title: Uncertainty-aware Language Guidance for Concept Bottleneck Models
summary: Concept Bottleneck Models (CBMs) provide inherent interpretability by first mapping input samples to high-level semantic concepts, followed by a combination of these concepts for the final classification. However, the annotation of human-understandable concepts requires extensive expert knowledge and labor, constraining the broad adoption of CBMs. On the other hand, there are a few works that leverage the knowledge of large language models (LLMs) to construct concept bottlenecks. Nevertheless, they face two essential limitations: First, they overlook the uncertainty associated with the concepts annotated by LLMs and lack a valid mechanism to quantify uncertainty about the annotated concepts, increasing the risk of errors due to hallucinations from LLMs. Additionally, they fail to incorporate the uncertainty associated with these annotations into the learning process for concept bottleneck models. To address these limitations, we propose a novel uncertainty-aware CBM method, which not only rigorously quantifies the uncertainty of LLM-annotated concept labels with valid and distribution-free guarantees, but also incorporates quantified concept uncertainty into the CBM training procedure to account for varying levels of reliability across LLM-annotated concepts. We also provide the theoretical analysis for our proposed method. Extensive experiments on the real-world datasets validate the desired properties of our proposed methods.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes an uncertainty-aware Concept Bottleneck Model (CBM) that quantifies uncertainty in LLM-annotated concepts with distribution-free guarantees and incorporates this uncertainty into CBM training to handle varying reliability of LLM annotations.
AI.motivation: Traditional CBMs require expensive expert annotations for concepts, limiting their adoption. While some works use LLMs to generate concepts, they overlook the uncertainty in LLM annotations and lack mechanisms to quantify or incorporate this uncertainty, increasing error risks from LLM hallucinations.
AI.method: Proposes a novel uncertainty-aware CBM method that: 1) rigorously quantifies uncertainty of LLM-annotated concept labels with valid distribution-free guarantees, and 2) incorporates quantified concept uncertainty into CBM training to account for varying reliability across LLM-annotated concepts. The method includes theoretical analysis.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on real-world datasets validate the desired properties of the proposed methods, showing improved handling of uncertain LLM annotations.
AI.conclusion: The proposed uncertainty-aware CBM addresses limitations of existing LLM-based concept annotation methods by providing rigorous uncertainty quantification and incorporating this uncertainty into model training, making CBMs more practical by reducing reliance on expensive expert annotations while managing LLM uncertainty.

title: CycleBEV: Regularizing View Transformation Networks via View Cycle Consistency for Bird's-Eye-View Semantic Segmentation
summary: Transforming image features from perspective view (PV) space to bird's-eye-view (BEV) space remains challenging in autonomous driving due to depth ambiguity and occlusion. Although several view transformation (VT) paradigms have been proposed, the challenge still remains. In this paper, we propose a new regularization framework, dubbed CycleBEV, that enhances existing VT models for BEV semantic segmentation. Inspired by cycle consistency, widely used in image distribution modeling, we devise an inverse view transformation (IVT) network that maps BEV segmentation maps back to PV segmentation maps and use it to regularize VT networks during training through cycle consistency losses, enabling them to capture richer semantic and geometric information from input PV images. To further exploit the capacity of the IVT network, we introduce two novel ideas that extend cycle consistency into geometric and representation spaces. We evaluate CycleBEV on four representative VT models covering three major paradigms using the large-scale nuScenes dataset. Experimental results show consistent improvements -- with gains of up to 0.74, 4.86, and 3.74 mIoU for drivable area, vehicle, and pedestrian classes, respectively -- without increasing inference complexity, since the IVT network is used only during training. The implementation code is available at https://github.com/JeongbinHong/CycleBEV.

AI.tldr: CycleBEV: A regularization framework using cycle consistency to enhance bird's-eye-view semantic segmentation by adding an inverse view transformation network during training.
AI.motivation: Transforming image features from perspective view to bird's-eye-view in autonomous driving is challenging due to depth ambiguity and occlusion. Existing view transformation paradigms still struggle with these issues, requiring better methods to capture semantic and geometric information.
AI.method: Proposes CycleBEV framework with an inverse view transformation network that maps BEV segmentation maps back to PV segmentation maps. Uses cycle consistency losses to regularize VT networks during training. Introduces two novel extensions of cycle consistency into geometric and representation spaces to further exploit IVT network capacity.
AI.result: Consistent improvements across four representative VT models on nuScenes dataset: gains up to 0.74, 4.86, and 3.74 mIoU for drivable area, vehicle, and pedestrian classes respectively. No inference complexity increase since IVT network is only used during training.
AI.conclusion: CycleBEV effectively enhances existing view transformation models for BEV semantic segmentation through cycle consistency regularization, improving performance without adding inference overhead. The framework demonstrates broad applicability across different VT paradigms.

title: AI Must Embrace Specialization via Superhuman Adaptable Intelligence
summary: Everyone from AI executives and researchers to doomsayers, politicians, and activists is talking about Artificial General Intelligence (AGI). Yet, they often don't seem to agree on its exact definition. One common definition of AGI is an AI that can do everything a human can do, but are humans truly general? In this paper, we address what's wrong with our conception of AGI, and why, even in its most coherent formulation, it is a flawed concept to describe the future of AI. We explore whether the most widely accepted definitions are plausible, useful, and truly general. We argue that AI must embrace specialization, rather than strive for generality, and in its specialization strive for superhuman performance, and introduce Superhuman Adaptable Intelligence (SAI). SAI is defined as intelligence that can learn to exceed humans at anything important that we can do, and that can fill in the skill gaps where humans are incapable. We then lay out how SAI can help hone a discussion around AI that was blurred by an overloaded definition of AGI, and extrapolate the implications of using it as a guide for the future.

AI.tldr: The paper critiques the flawed concept of AGI (Artificial General Intelligence) and proposes SAI (Superhuman Adaptable Intelligence) as a more useful framework for AI development, emphasizing specialization over generality.
AI.motivation: Current discussions about AGI are confused by overloaded and inconsistent definitions. The human-centric "can do everything a human can do" definition is problematic because humans themselves are not truly general. The paper aims to clarify AI discourse by addressing what's wrong with AGI concepts and proposing a better framework.
AI.method: The authors analyze the coherence and usefulness of widely accepted AGI definitions, questioning whether they are plausible, useful, and truly general. They then introduce the concept of SAI (Superhuman Adaptable Intelligence) as an alternative framework that emphasizes specialization and superhuman performance.
